column1: What is a process in an operating system?
column2: A running program with its own address space, file descriptor table, and PCB.

column1: What is a thread in an operating system?
column2: A unit of execution within a process that shares the address space and open files, but has its own stack and TCB.

column1: What is a PCB?
column2: A Process Control Block, a kernel structure per process containing items such as PID, state, memory map, file descriptor table, and exit status.

column1: What is a TCB?
column2: A Thread Control Block, a per-thread structure containing registers, stack pointer, and scheduling information.

column1: What is a context switch?
column2: Saving the current thread's CPU state and loading another thread's state.

column1: Why is a context switch expensive?
column2: Because it may flush the TLB and cool the CPU caches.

column1: What is a zombie process?
column2: A terminated child whose parent has not called wait yet, so the kernel keeps its exit status.

column1: What is an orphan process?
column2: A running child whose parent has exited, so it is adopted by init, PID 1.

column1: What is preemption?
column2: The OS forcibly taking the CPU away from a running process or thread, usually via a timer interrupt.

column1: What is starvation in scheduling?
column2: A runnable process never gets CPU time because the scheduler keeps favoring others.

column1: What is the convoy effect?
column2: Short jobs wait behind a long job, a classic problem of FCFS scheduling.

column1: What are the three ways the OS is activated?
What is turnaround time?
What is waiting time?
What is response time?
column2: First run time minus arrival time.

column1: What metric measures jobs completed per unit time?
What is CPU utilization?
Which non-preemptive scheduler minimizes average turnaround time?
Which preemptive scheduler minimizes average turnaround time?
Which scheduler tends to minimize response time at the cost of turnaround?
What can user mode not do?
column2: Execute privileged instructions or access all memory freely.

column1: What can kernel mode do?
column2: Access hardware fully and run OS code.

column1: What is an interrupt?
column2: An asynchronous hardware event such as a timer or I O signal that transfers control to the OS.

column1: What is an exception?
column2: A synchronous CPU fault such as a page fault, divide by zero, or general protection fault.

column1: What is a system call?
column2: An explicit trap from user mode into the kernel to request an OS service.

column1: What often dominates cost in switching-heavy workloads?
What does wait NULL do?
What does waitpid pid do?
column2: Waits for a specific child process.

column1: What should every fork typically be matched by?
column2: A wait call or a SIGCHLD handler, otherwise zombies can leak.

column1: What is copy on write for fork?
column2: Parent and child initially share read-only pages and a page is copied only when one side writes to it.

column1: Why is naive fork wasteful?
column2: Because it would copy the entire address space even when the child often calls exec immediately.

column1: What happens first on a copy on write write access?
column2: The CPU raises a protection fault exception.

column1: What is the main benefit of copy on write when the child execs immediately?
column2: Zero pages need to be copied.

column1: In a many-to-one ULT model, what does the kernel see?
What is a key disadvantage of many-to-one ULT?
column2: A blocking system call can block the whole process.

column1: Does many-to-one ULT allow true parallelism on multicore?
Does one-to-one KLT allow true parallelism on multicore?
What is the main tradeoff of one-to-one KLT?
column2: Independent blocking and real parallelism, but thread management has system call overhead.

column1: Is it true that single-core CPUs do not need separate TCBs for threads?
Is switching to a different process faster than switching to a user-level thread in the same process?
column2: No, process switches are slower because the address space changes and TLB and caches are affected.

column1: How is the OS activated according to the 2022 final question?
column2: By interrupts, exceptions, and system calls.

column1: A user process accesses a virtual address not present in its address space. Is that an interrupt, exception, system call, or none?
A periodic timer fires and activates the scheduler. Is that an interrupt, exception, system call, or none?
A user process adds two CPU registers. Is that an interrupt, exception, system call, or none?
A user process creates a new file with open. Is that an interrupt, exception, system call, or none?
A user program divides by zero using the Intel DIV instruction. Is that an interrupt, exception, system call, or none?
A user process writes to an anonymous pipe with write. Is that an interrupt, exception, system call, or none?
A user process reads from a present virtual address on its heap. Is that an interrupt, exception, system call, or none?
The network controller signals a newly arrived packet. Is that an interrupt, exception, system call, or none?
A user process tries to write CR3 in user mode. Is that an interrupt, exception, system call, or none?
A user process creates a new process with fork. Is that an interrupt, exception, system call, or none?
A user program performs an integer ADD instruction. Is that an interrupt, exception, system call, or none?
What happens if a privileged CPU instruction is executed in user mode?
column2: The CPU raises an exception, typically a general protection fault, and control transfers to the kernel.

column1: Give two examples of privileged CPU instructions.
column2: Examples include CLI or STI, IN or OUT, HLT, and loading control registers such as CR3.

column1: What is cooperative multitasking?
column2: The running process must yield explicitly or block before another process runs.

column1: What is preemptive multitasking?
column2: The OS uses a timer interrupt to forcibly context switch the running process.

column1: Which style do real operating systems like Linux and Windows use?
Which PCB versus TCB statement is true: open files are shared by all threads or private per thread?
column2: Open files are shared by all threads and belong to the process PCB.

column1: Which PCB versus TCB statement is true: CPU registers are shared by all threads or private per thread?
column2: CPU registers are private per thread and belong in the TCB.

column1: Which PCB versus TCB statement is true: process exit status is shared by all threads or private per thread?
column2: The process exit status belongs to the process PCB.

column1: Which PCB versus TCB statement is false: the code and the stack are both shared by all threads?
column2: False, the code is shared but each thread has its own stack.

column1: What does fork do?
column2: It creates a new process that is an almost identical copy of the current process in a separate address space.

column1: What value does fork return in the child?
What value does fork return in the parent?
In the test exam process tree, what becomes the parent of PID 20 after PID 10 exits?
In the test exam process tree, what is the parent of PID 23?
In the test exam process tree, what value does wait return in PID 20?
In the test exam process tree, what is the state of PID 22 after wait reaps it?
column2: N, meaning it is gone or reaped.

column1: In the test exam process tree, what is the state of PID 23 running less?
To run a child program while keeping the parent alive, what must you usually do?
column2: Fork first and then call exec in the child.

column1: Does exec return a new PID?
column2: No, exec keeps the same PID.

column1: When does exec return to the caller?
Does exec always close all open file descriptors?
column2: No, only descriptors marked close on exec are closed.

column1: In the 2024 retake code trace, does execvp of cp run perror on success?
column2: No, perror runs only if execvp fails.

column1: In the 2024 retake code trace, does the parent wait for the child?
In the 2024 retake code trace, what PID does printf print in the parent?
In the 2022 RR example with quantum 1 and P1 3, P2 1, P3 2, P4 2, what is the average turnaround time?
In the 2022 RR example with quantum 1 and P1 3, P2 1, P3 2, P4 2, what schedule runs from t0 to t7?
In the 2022 non-preemptive SJF example with the same four processes, what is the average turnaround time?
In the 2022 non-preemptive SJF example with the same four processes, what schedule runs from t0 to t7?
In the 2024 FCFS example with arrivals P2 at 0, P4 at 1, P3 at 2, P1 at 3, what is the average turnaround time?
In the 2024 FCFS example, what schedule runs from t0 to t9?
In the 2024 SRTF example, what schedule runs from t0 to t9?
In the 2024 SRTF example, what is the average turnaround time?
In the test exam with P1 at 2 for 5, P2 at 1 for 4, P3 at 3 for 1, what is the preemptive SJF schedule from t1 to t10?
In the same test exam, what is the RR schedule with quantum 1 from t1 to t10?
In the 2024 retake RR example, what schedule runs from t0 to t9?
In the 2024 retake RR example, what is the average turnaround time?
Which scheduling algorithm is fair and avoids starvation but is not optimal for average turnaround?
Which scheduling algorithm requires a priori knowledge of burst times to minimize average wait and turnaround?
Which scheduling algorithm approximates SJF using feedback from past behavior?
Which scheduling algorithm is simple but suffers from the convoy effect?
What is the goal of MLFQ?
column2: Approximate SJF without knowing burst times in advance and give interactive jobs good response time.

column1: In MLFQ, where does a new job start?
In MLFQ, what happens when a job uses up its allotment at a level?
In MLFQ, what does the periodic priority boost do?
column2: Moves jobs back to the top queue to prevent starvation and handle phase changes.

column1: What is the page size formula from offset bits OB?
column2: Two to the power of OB bytes.

column1: What is the number of page table entries from VPN bits NB?
column2: Two to the power of NB entries.

column1: What is the page table size formula?
column2: Number of entries times bytes per entry.

column1: How do you compute effective access time with a TLB for a single-level page table?
column2: EAT equals h times tT plus tM plus one minus h times tT plus 2 times tM.

column1: How do you compute page fault EAT?
column2: EAT equals one minus p times tM plus p times tF.

column1: Why can a tiny page fault probability still dominate EAT?
column2: Because page fault service time is much larger than normal memory access time.

column1: What problem does the TLB solve?
column2: It avoids the many memory accesses needed for page table walks on every reference.

column1: What is a TLB?
column2: A small hardware cache of recently used virtual page number to physical frame number translations.

column1: What happens on a TLB hit?
column2: The PFN is returned quickly and the access proceeds.

column1: What happens on a TLB miss?
column2: The hardware or OS walks the page table, installs the translation in the TLB, and retries.

column1: What does ASID or PCID help avoid?
column2: Full TLB flushes on context switch.

column1: How do larger pages affect TLB coverage?
column2: Each TLB entry covers more memory, so fewer entries are needed.

column1: What is demand paging?
column2: Pages are loaded into RAM only when first accessed.

column1: What happens if a faulting virtual address is not legal for the process?
column2: The OS delivers SIGSEGV and the process usually dies.

column1: What happens if a needed page was evicted but is still a valid address?
column2: The OS finds a frame, loads the page from disk, updates the PTE, and retries the instruction.

column1: What is thrashing?
column2: The system spends more time swapping pages than doing useful work.

column1: What causes thrashing?
column2: The combined working set of active processes exceeds RAM.

column1: What is a working set?
column2: The set of pages referenced in the last delta time units.

column1: How can the OS mitigate thrashing?
column2: Reduce load, use local or working-set-aware replacement, or add more RAM.

column1: What is Belady's anomaly?
column2: With FIFO, adding more frames can sometimes increase the number of page faults.

column1: Which page replacement algorithms are stack algorithms that do not exhibit Belady's anomaly?
What does the present or valid bit in a PTE mean?
column2: Whether the page is resident in RAM.

column1: Which PTE bit is used by CLOCK?
Which PTE bit shows whether a page must be written back on eviction?
What does the NX bit do?
Which replacement algorithm evicts the page used farthest in the future?
Why is OPT not feasible in a real OS?
column2: Because the OS does not know future page references.

column1: Which replacement algorithm evicts the oldest loaded page?
Which replacement algorithm evicts the least recently used page?
Which replacement algorithm uses a reference bit and a circular scan?
Which replacement algorithm evicts a uniformly random page?
What is external fragmentation?
column2: Enough total free memory exists, but it is split into pieces too small to satisfy a request.

column1: What is internal fragmentation?
column2: Unused memory inside an allocated unit because the allocation granularity is larger than the request.

column1: Does paging eliminate external fragmentation?
column2: Yes, fixed-size pages eliminate external fragmentation.

column1: What fragmentation does paging introduce?
How do huge pages trade off fragmentation and translation overhead?
column2: They increase internal fragmentation but reduce TLB misses and shrink page tables.

column1: What is first fit in a heap allocator?
column2: Return the first free block large enough for the request.

column1: What is next fit in a heap allocator?
column2: Like first fit, but continue scanning from where the last search stopped.

column1: What is best fit in a heap allocator?
column2: Return the smallest free block that fits.

column1: What is worst fit in a heap allocator?
What is coalescing in a heap allocator?
column2: Merging adjacent free blocks when memory is freed.

column1: What is the buddy allocator?
column2: An allocator using power-of-two block sizes with buddy blocks that can split and merge efficiently.

column1: What is a main drawback of the buddy allocator?
column2: It can waste a lot of space through internal fragmentation.

column1: Why can Java or Go compact heaps more easily than C?
column2: They track pointers, while C does not have runtime metadata for all pointers.

column1: What is temporal locality?
column2: Recently accessed data is likely to be used again soon.

column1: What is spatial locality?
column2: Data near recently accessed data is likely to be used soon.

column1: What is a cache line?
column2: The unit of transfer between RAM and cache, typically 64 bytes on x86.

column1: What are the three Cs of cache misses?
What is write-through caching?
column2: Every write updates both the cache and memory.

column1: What is write-back caching?
column2: Writes update the cache and memory is updated only when the line is evicted.

column1: What extra metadata does a write-back cache need?
What is PIPT cache indexing and tagging?
What is VIPT cache indexing and tagging?
What is VIVT cache indexing and tagging?
Which cache design is common for L1 data caches?
Which cache design is common for L2 and L3 caches?
Which cache design suffers from both homonyms and synonyms?
Is PIVT a common real-world cache design?
Do PIPT caches need invalidation on every context switch?
Can VIPT caches begin indexing in parallel with TLB lookup?
Do write-back caches require a dirty bit per line?
What does Meltdown exploit to leak kernel data?
column2: Out-of-order execution plus a cache side channel.

column1: What is the main mitigation for Meltdown mentioned in the slides?
What does Spectre exploit?
What is a named file-backed page mapping?
column2: A page whose content comes from a file, such as text segments, data segments, shared libraries, or mmap files.

column1: What is an anonymous page mapping?
column2: A page with no file backing, such as heap, stack, or MAP_ANONYMOUS memory.

column1: How is a clean file-backed page evicted?
column2: It can simply be dropped and later reread from the file.

column1: How is an anonymous page evicted?
column2: It must be written to swap before eviction.

column1: What are shadow page tables in virtualization?
column2: Hypervisor-maintained tables that directly map guest virtual addresses to host physical addresses.

column1: What is a key cost of shadow page tables?
column2: The hypervisor must trap guest page-table updates to keep shadows in sync.

column1: What are EPT and NPT?
column2: Hardware nested page tables that let the CPU perform guest and host translations.

column1: What is a key cost of nested paging with EPT or NPT?
column2: A TLB miss can require many memory accesses to walk both translation trees.

column1: If the page offset has 12 bits, what is the page size?
If the page offset has 16 bits, what is the page size?
If the page offset has 21 bits, what is the page size?
With 20 VPN bits in a single-level page table, how many entries are there?
With 20 VPN bits and 4 bytes per entry, how large is the page table?
With a 4 MiB page table and 4 KiB pages, how many pages are needed to store it?

In the same 2022 four-level paging question, what is the virtual address size?

In that same two-level page table question, how much present virtual memory is mapped?
In the same question, is virtual address 0x00001050 valid?
In the same question, what physical address does virtual address 0x00001050 translate to?
If a process accesses a not-present address that is not valid for the process, what happens?
column2: The OS delivers SIGSEGV, usually terminating the process.

column1: If a process accesses a not-present but valid text page that was evicted, what does the OS do?
column2: Finds a frame, reads the page from disk, updates the PTE, and retries the faulting instruction.

column1: Why is a single-level page table impractical for the 48-bit VA example with 8-byte entries?
column2: It would need 2 to the 36 entries, about 512 GiB per process.

column1: How does Linux manage page table memory more efficiently than a single-level page table?
column2: By using multi-level hierarchical page tables and allocating page-table pages only for mapped regions.

column1: For OB equals 21, what is the page size in the 2024 linear page table formula slide?
For NB equals 27, how many page table entries are there?
Are large pages an advantage or disadvantage for internal fragmentation?
Are large pages an advantage or disadvantage for page table size?
What impact do larger pages have on the TLB?
column2: They increase TLB coverage and reduce misses for the same memory footprint.

column1: With 5 paging levels and 9 index bits per level plus a 12-bit offset, how wide is the virtual address?
Why is the physical address width 52 bits in the 5-level example?
column2: A 64-bit PTE uses 12 low bits for flags, leaving 52 bits for the physical frame number.

column1: In a 64-bit CPU with 64 GB RAM, which page table variant is the best practical choice according to the quiz slide?
In the 4-level paging minimum page-table pages example for a 4 MiB process, how many page-table pages are needed?
In the hashed inverted page table size example with 1 GiB RAM and 4 KiB pages, how large is the table?
How large is an inverted page table relative to processes and virtual memory usage?
column2: Its size depends on the number of physical frames, not the number of processes or the amount of virtual memory.

column1: In the 2024 segmentation VA to PA question, what does 0x30008 translate to?
In the 2024 segmentation VA to PA question, what happens to 0x12345?
In the 2024 segmentation VA to PA question, what does 0x22222 translate to?
In the 2024 segmentation PA to VA question, what virtual address maps to physical address 0x21234?
In the 2024 segmentation PA to VA question, what is the result for physical address 0x12345?
In the 2024 segmentation PA to VA question, what virtual address maps to physical address 0x20002?
In the 2022 LRU example with 4 frames and references 4, 2, 1, 3, 5 after t0, how many faults occur from t1 to t5?
In the RANDOM replacement slide, is the exact table deterministic?
column2: No, the outcome depends on random evictions.

column1: In the CLOCK worked example with reference string 1, 2, 3, 1, 4, 2, 5 and 3 frames, how many page faults occur?
What does CLOCK do when it sees a frame with reference bit 1 during eviction scan?
column2: It clears the bit to 0 and gives the page a second chance.

column1: What does CLOCK do when it sees a frame with reference bit 0 during eviction scan?
Is FIFO susceptible to Belady's anomaly?
Is CLOCK also called second chance?
Does MRU evict the most recently used page?
Should a good replacement algorithm maximize page faults?
Does FIFO guarantee the fewest page faults?
Is RANDOM avoided in practice because it is computationally expensive?
Does LRU consider the least recently used page for eviction?
Is external fragmentation a problem for variable-size allocators but not fixed-size page allocators?
Can internal fragmentation be a disadvantage of huge pages?
What is a race condition?
column2: Concurrent access to shared data where the final result depends on interleaving and at least one access is a write.

column1: What is a critical section?
column2: A code region that must be executed by at most one thread at a time.

column1: What three properties must a correct critical section implementation provide?
column2: Mutual exclusion, progress, and bounded waiting.

column1: Why can counter increment cause a race condition?
column2: Because it compiles into multiple instructions such as load, increment, and store.

column1: What are the four Coffman conditions for deadlock?
column2: Mutual exclusion, hold and wait, no preemption, and circular wait.

column1: How can deadlock be prevented?
column2: Break at least one Coffman condition by design.

column1: What is deadlock avoidance?
column2: Grant resources only if the resulting state stays safe, for example with Banker's algorithm.

column1: What is deadlock detection and recovery?
column2: Allow deadlocks, detect them later, then recover by killing a process or forcing preemption.

column1: What is the ostrich algorithm for deadlocks?
column2: Ignore deadlocks and rely on rebooting or similar recovery.

column1: What is priority inversion?
column2: A high-priority thread waits on a lock held by a low-priority thread while a medium-priority thread preempts the low-priority one.

column1: How is priority inversion fixed?
What is livelock?
column2: Threads keep running but make no useful progress.

column1: How is livelock different from deadlock?
column2: In livelock, threads are not blocked, they are active but ineffective.

column1: What is a mutex?
column2: A binary lock with ownership, used to protect a critical section.

column1: What is a binary semaphore?
column2: A 0 or 1 synchronization primitive without ownership semantics.

column1: What is a counting semaphore?
column2: A semaphore with a nonnegative integer value used to model a limited number of resources.

column1: What is a condition variable?
column2: A primitive used with a mutex so threads can sleep until a condition becomes true.

column1: What is a monitor?
column2: A language-level construct combining mutual exclusion with built-in condition variables.

column1: What is the rule of thumb for mutex versus CV versus counting semaphore?
column2: Use a mutex for mutual exclusion, a CV to wait for state changes, and a counting semaphore to limit resource count.

column1: What hardware operations are spin locks built on?
column2: Atomic read-modify-write instructions such as test-and-set or compare-and-swap.

column1: Do spin locks require kernel mode or privileged instructions?
column2: No, user-space atomic instructions are enough.

column1: In the pipe API, what is fd 0 and fd 1 after pipe fd?
column2: fd 0 is the read end and fd 1 is the write end.

column1: Why must every unused pipe end be closed?
column2: Otherwise the reader may never observe EOF.

column1: What does dup2 old new do?
column2: It replaces file descriptor new with a duplicate of old.

column1: In the correct getTicket implementation, what ticket values can three concurrent threads get?
Why is the correct getTicket implementation synchronized correctly?
column2: The lock protects the increment and the local variable captures the value before unlock.

column1: What are the two bugs in the 2022 buggy getTicket code?
column2: The unlock uses the wrong mutex name and the function returns lastTicket outside the critical section.

column1: Give one possible bad outcome from the buggy getTicket code.
column2: An outcome such as 3, 3, 3 can occur.

column1: What are the three bugs in the 2024 my_malloc code?
column2: The return type should be void pointer, returning while holding the lock causes deadlock, and allocate_block runs after unlock causing a race.

column1: What are the two bugs in the 2024 retake my_malloc code?
column2: find_free_block runs before taking the lock and returning NULL while holding the lock leaks the lock.

column1: For a very short critical section like getTicket, which primitive minimizes overhead best?
Are spin locks efficient with low contention and short critical sections?
What is a spin lock?
column2: A lock where the waiting thread busy-waits in a loop checking for availability.

column1: Are spin locks mainly useful on single-core CPUs?
column2: No, they are mainly useful on multi-core CPUs.

column1: Do blocking locks require a system call to put a thread to sleep?
Do anonymous pipes need application-level locks around read and write?
column2: No, the kernel pipe buffer already handles synchronization.

column1: Why is a mutex alone not enough to implement a bounded buffer efficiently?
column2: Consumers or producers would have to busy-wait because a mutex cannot put them to sleep until the condition changes.

column1: What extra primitive solves the bounded buffer problem efficiently?
In the semaphore bounded buffer, what should empty start at for a buffer of size 3?
In the semaphore bounded buffer, what should full start at?
In the semaphore bounded buffer, what should mutex start at?
Are anonymous pipes based on shared memory?
Do POSIX message queues provide bidirectional communication on the same system?
Do named pipes work across different hosts over a network?
column2: No, sockets are needed for network communication.

column1: Can accessing shared memory without synchronization cause races?
After setup, does shared memory IPC allow direct memory access without system calls on each access?
Must message-passing APIs use an explicit mutex on every send and receive?
column2: No, synchronization is handled by the IPC mechanism.

column1: Are anonymous pipes message-passing IPC?
Are anonymous pipes bidirectional by default?
Are POSIX message queues for different hosts over a network?
Does fork automatically close inherited anonymous pipe file descriptors in the child?
Does shared-memory IPC with a linked list require synchronization?
column2: Yes, otherwise the shared data structure can be corrupted.

column1: What minimum additions create a deadlock in the 2022 RAG question?
column2: Add P2 waiting for R3 and assign R3 to P1, creating the cycle P1 to R2 to P2 to R3 to P1.

column1: Is the 2024 RAG example deadlocked?
For a resource allocation graph with single-instance resources, when does deadlock exist?
column2: If and only if the graph contains a cycle.

column1: In the readers writers test exam, under reader preference, which arrives before writer w1 gets a turn?
In the readers writers test exam, under writer preference, what order occurs after r1 and r2 finish?
column2: w1, w2, w3, then r3 and r4.

column1: What is polling in I O?
column2: The CPU repeatedly checks a device status register.

column1: What is interrupt-driven I O?
column2: The CPU starts the request and the device interrupts when it finishes.

column1: What is DMA?
column2: A controller transfers data directly between device and RAM and interrupts only when the transfer completes.

column1: Does DMA eliminate interrupts entirely?
column2: No, it usually still causes one interrupt at the end of the transfer.

column1: When is polling sensible?
column2: For very fast devices or when latency matters more than CPU efficiency.

column1: Why is DMA essential for high-bandwidth devices?
column2: Because it avoids per-word CPU involvement.

column1: Can disabling interrupts on one core help avoid deadlock with a spin lock and an interrupt handler on that same core?
Does disabling interrupts on one core solve the problem across all cores?
On an interrupt, does the CPU save the current context before running the handler?
What do rwx bits mean on a regular file?
column2: r means read contents, w means modify contents, and x means execute the file.

column1: What do rwx bits mean on a directory?
column2: r lists names, w creates renames or deletes entries, and x allows traversal.

column1: What does the sticky bit on a directory do?
column2: Only the file owner or root can delete entries there even if the directory is writable.

column1: To delete a file, what permissions matter most?
column2: Write and execute on the parent directory, not write permission on the file itself.

Which file allocation method has O n random access because you must walk the chain?
Which file allocation method supports sparse files efficiently?
What metadata does a per-process file descriptor table entry point to?
What does the system-wide open file table store?
column2: Current offset, access mode, and a pointer to the inode.

column1: What does the in-memory inode table store?
column2: File metadata such as size, permissions, block pointers, and reference count.

column1: What does dup fd imply about file offsets?
column2: The duplicated descriptors share the same open-file-table entry and therefore the same file offset.

column1: What happens to file offsets across fork?
column2: Parent and child share open-file entries and therefore share offsets.

column1: What is the HDD block read time formula?
column2: Seek time plus rotational latency plus transfer time.

column1: At 7200 RPM, what is the average rotational latency?
